nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to the Philippines (Cebu and Luzon), where it forms large colonies in shallow, fast-flowing freshwater rivers in lowland forests, rooting via stolons in sandy to gravelly riverbeds; the only Schismatoglottis species known to grow as a fully aquatic plant

decorPreferences: loose sandy or fine-gravel substrate that the stolons can run through
nutrient-rich aqua soil or inert substrate supplemented with a root tab
moderate flow to mimic the species' fast-flowing river habitat
may also anchor to porous hardscape (lava rock, driftwood) but does not attach with adhesive roots like Bucephalandra or Anubias
stable, established tank — acclimate slowly to avoid melt

goodWith: shrimp (Neocaridina and Caridina) which graze biofilm without harming the plant
other low-light foreground/midground species such as Cryptocoryne parva, Bucephalandra and Anubias nana
avoidWith: large herbivorous fish (goldfish, silver dollars, large plecos)
vigorous diggers (large cichlids, geophagines) that will uproot the runners
newly set up tanks with unstable parameters

imageCandidates.notes: Product page features cultivated submerged specimen showing characteristic almond-shaped green leaves and dense substrate-rooted stand.
Product page with multiple submerged images showing growth habit and leaf morphology.

summary: Prieto's Schismatoglottis is a small, fully aquatic aroid endemic to lowland rivers of Cebu and Luzon in the Philippines. Known scientifically as Schismatoglottis prietoi, it is the only fully aquatic member of an otherwise terrestrial genus, and unlike related aroids, it roots into the substrate and spreads by runners rather than anchoring to hardscape.
careNotes: Prieto's Schismatoglottis forms broad colonies in fast-flowing lowland rivers on Cebu and Luzon in the Philippines, rooting into sandy to gravelly riverbeds via creeping stolons. It sits within the same family as Bucephalandra — both belong to tribe Schismatoglottideae in the aroid family Araceae — but the wider genus Schismatoglottis consists almost entirely of terrestrial rheophytes (plants adapted to life on rocks in or beside fast-moving water that grow out of the water rather than in it). S. prietoi is the sole exception: the only species in the genus known to grow fully submerged in nature. In the aquarium, that distinction matters: this plant is kept as a substrate-rooted foreground or midground species, not attached to hardscape the way Bucephalandra or Anubias are. The creeping rhizome (the thick horizontal stem that anchors the plant) and its lateral runners are pressed into a loose sandy or fine-gravel substrate, or into nutrient-rich aqua soil. Over time, the runners produce daughter plantlets along their length, eventually building a dense, spreading stand somewhat reminiscent of a small Cryptocoryne. Individual leaves are typically 3–8 cm long and 1–2 cm wide; plants reach around 2–8 cm in height. Lighting is flexible. Low light is sufficient for survival, but medium light tends to produce fuller, denser growth and better leaf color. High light without supplemental CO2 is generally not recommended, as it tends to encourage algae on the plant's slow-growing leaves without proportionally speeding up the plant itself. CO2 injection is not required, but it does accelerate establishment noticeably. Growth starts slowly while the plant transitions from the emersed (above-water) form typically sold in nurseries to its submerged growth habit; once the runners become active it generally outpaces Anubias and Bucephalandra in spread. Root tabs placed near the rhizome are commonly reported to improve results in inert substrates. As with other aroids sold in emersed form, sudden shifts in temperature, pH, or water hardness during acclimation can trigger leaf melt — where the original leaves die back before new submerged leaves emerge. Introducing it only into a stable, established aquarium and acclimating gradually reduces this risk; new leaves typically push from the rhizome within a few weeks. The plant can also be positioned in crevices of porous lava rock or driftwood and will anchor its roots there, but because it does not attach with adhesive holdfast roots the way Bucephalandra and Anubias do, substrate planting tends to be the more secure long-term approach.
breedingNotes: Propagation in the hobby is vegetative. The plant produces lateral runners (stolons) that generate daughter plantlets along the substrate; once a young plantlet has developed its own roots and 2–3 leaves, it can be snipped from the parent runner with sharp scissors and replanted elsewhere. Mature rhizomes can also be divided in the same way as Bucephalandra — cut at a natural bend, ensuring each division retains several leaves and visible roots. Reproduction via the aroid flower structure (a spathe and spadix) is not a practical method in the home aquarium.

primary.name: Aquasabi
primary.notes: Primary source. Aquasabi profile provides temperature (20-28 °C), pH (6-8), low-to-medium lighting, CO2 not required, slow-then-faster growth, foreground-to-midground placement, substrate-rooted with stolons (anchors in porous rock but not adhesive epiphyte), recommended 5-20 cm aquarium height, easy care, and Cebu Philippines origin.
